reasoning: All four reach the correct verdict and catch the stacked correlation-causation-supplement leaps, so discrimination comes from execution. C wins: it frames four clean competing hypotheses (including artifact/garden-of-forking-paths), runs the tightest explicit Bayesian chain (prior 15-20%, low diagnosticity because the result is equally likely under H2/H3, posterior 20-25%), names a mechanism (adenosine, dopamine), notes adjustment never fully removes confounding, and flags the invented dose-claim, while staying readable. B is nearly as strong and arguably the most quantitatively explicit (prior, an actual likelihood-ratio of ~1.5-2x, posterior, plus the sophisticated healthy/sick-abstainer bias and Mendelian-randomization reference), edging out the rest on pure statistical rigor but offering slightly thinner hypothesis structure. D has the best bias_awareness by far, the only answer with a true selective-skeptic self-check (the asymmetry tell) plus follow-the-incentive reasoning, but it gives no numeric posterior, which costs it on calibration and drops it below the Bayesian pair. A is solid and correctly handles relative-vs-absolute risk and base rates, but it offers the least depth (no real Bayesian mechanics, weakest self-bias check, and a minor "five stories" miscount), placing it last.
